Council, this case asks a single, concrete question under a clear timing bar: Did Polymarket, on or before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 Pacific Standard Time, launch an official fungible token and publicly communicate that launch through its own official channels, with NFTs, promotional giveaways, tokenized point systems, and partner/community co-launches expressly excluded?

Our theory of the case
- Polymarket did launch an official fungible token within the period and publicly tied itself to that launch through its own channels. The evidence will show an on-chain fungible asset attributable to Polymarket and a contemporaneous, official communication acknowledging and describing that launch.

What the dispute really turns on
This is not a referendum on integrations (e.g., payments in stablecoins), third-party assets used on the platform, or informal community efforts. The dispute turns on four conjunctive elements:
1) Fungibility: The asset must be a transferable, on-chain, fungible token (e.g., a standard like ERC‑20 or equivalent), not an NFT, not non-transferable “points,” and not an app-internal balance without on-chain transfer between independent wallets.
2) Official launch by Polymarket: The originating act must be attributable to Polymarket (the operator/brand) as issuer/launcher—e.g., by deploying or commissioning deployment under its authority, controlling mint parameters, and/or assuming responsibility for the token as its own product.
3) Public communication via official channels: Polymarket must have publicly communicated the token’s launch through channels it controls (e.g., its primary website, official blog, verified social accounts, product announcements, investor or policy disclosures). Mere press coverage or third-party chatter is not enough without Polymarket’s own on-record communication.
4) Timing: The launch and the official communication must fall on or before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST (which is 2027‑01‑01 08:00 UTC). Ambiguity in timestamps must be resolved to this bar.

Exclusions that do not satisfy the proposition
- NFTs, promotional claim codes, loyalty/XP/points (including tokenized points lacking open transferability), or speculative placeholders without on-chain, user-to-user transfer.
- “Community” or partner/brand co-launches where the token is not clearly Polymarket’s own official product, even if Polymarket participated. Exchange listings, liquidity pools, or market-maker arrangements do not transform a third-party asset into Polymarket’s token.

Factual questions for decision
- Identity: Is there a specific, identifiable on-chain fungible token that Polymarket held out as its own?
- Issuer control: Do the deployer/multisig/signers and contract provenance tie back to Polymarket as the launching entity?
- Official communication: Did Polymarket itself, through its official channels, publicly describe or announce the token as launched (name/ticker/contract or equivalent defining details)?
- Temporal placement: Do trusted, dated artifacts place both the launch and the official communication on or before the PST time bar?
- Exclusion screening: Does the asset fall into any excluded category (NFTs, points, promos, or a partner/community token)?

What proof would satisfy the burden (preponderance of the evidence)
Any combination of the following, weighed together, that makes it more likely than not that all four elements are met:
- A dated, official Polymarket communication expressly announcing the token’s launch and identifying it as Polymarket’s token.
- On-chain evidence linking the token’s deployment/mint to a wallet, multisig, or signer set attributable to Polymarket (e.g., via prior official disclosures, audits, or consistent use across releases).
- Product, legal, or policy materials published by Polymarket that characterize the asset as its token and place the event within the time bar.
- Archival captures that corroborate dates and content of official pages or posts, with consistent identifiers (name/ticker/contract) across sources.

What proof would defeat the proposition
- Clear, dated, official denials by Polymarket that it launched any token during the period; or unequivocal statements that any circulating asset is not its official token.
- Conclusive classification of the referenced asset as an NFT, a non-transferable/tokenized points system, a mere promotional voucher, or a partner/community token not officially launched by Polymarket.
- Absence of any official Polymarket communication acknowledging a token launch within the time bar, despite extensive search of official channels and archives.
- Mismatch between claimed identifiers (name/ticker/contract) and any asset Polymarket publicly embraced as its own.

Planned evidentiary approach
- Authenticate the set of Polymarket’s official channels and corporate entities for the relevant period.
- Collect dated official announcements, product pages, and policy/legal documents referencing any token.
- Link on-chain contract provenance to Polymarket-controlled keys or attested deployers.
- Use reliable archival and on-chain timestamping to anchor all events to PST.
- Screen out excluded categories and any collaborative/co-branded launches.

Relief sought
A finding that, by a preponderance of the evidence, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch on or before the PST deadline described above.

- Resolve contract provenance to Polymarket‑controlled keys or commissioned deployers.
- Fix all times to PST with archival and on‑chain records.
- Rigorously screen for exclusions and co‑branding.

Requested finding
On the completed record, find that Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, under a preponderance standard.

Council, the proposition is exacting and time‑barred: before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, did Polymarket “launch an official fungible token” and publicly communicate that launch through its own channels? Under a preponderance standard, the proponent must show it is more likely than not that each element is met. Our theory is straightforward: Polymarket did not launch—and did not officially communicate the launch of—any such token within the window. Any artifacts the proponent points to will either (a) be excluded by the definition (NFTs, promotions, tokenized points/XP, or community/partner tokens), (b) lack Polymarket’s official communication of a launch, or (c) fail the timing requirement.

Working definitions to guide decision
- Official fungible token: An on‑chain, user‑transferable fungible asset (ERC‑20‑type or equivalent) that Polymarket itself holds out as its own token—by name/ticker/contract—and for which Polymarket accepts responsibility or control. Mere platform usage of third‑party assets is not enough.
- Launch: Transition from internal/testing to public availability. A launch requires the contract to be live and publicly usable, with identifiable contract/address details. Teasers, roadmap references, or “coming soon” do not qualify.
- Publicly communicated through official channels: A clear, dated communication on channels Polymarket controls (primary website/domain, official blog or docs, verified social feeds, in‑app notices, or policy/product pages) that identifies the token as Polymarket’s and states or unmistakably implies it is launched/available. Third‑party press, community posts, or speculative commentary do not suffice.
- Exclusions (by the proposition): NFTs; promotional coupons/vouchers; point/XP/loyalty systems even if tokenized or partially transferable; and tokens launched “in collaboration with other projects or communities” where Polymarket is not the issuer or where the launch is co‑branded such that the token is not held out as Polymarket’s own.
- Timing: Both the launch and the qualifying official communication must occur on or before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST. Ambiguity in timestamps, time zones, or archival captures cannot carry the proponent’s burden.

Disputed questions likely to decide the case
1) Identity: Is there a specific fungible token Polymarket held out as its own—name/ticker and contract address—before the deadline?
2) Issuer/control: Do on‑chain deployer/owner/admin keys map to Polymarket‑controlled custody (e.g., known multisigs or attested addresses), rather than an unrelated deployer, partner, or community actor?
3) Official speech: Did Polymarket’s own channels explicitly announce or clearly characterize the token as launched within the window, as opposed to discussing features, rewards, or future plans?
4) Transferability/availability: Was the asset actually transferable by users on‑chain at launch, or was it a gated/internal balance, a claim on future issuance, or a non‑transferable points system?
5) Temporal placement: Do verifiable records place both the on‑chain launch and the official communication before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, after converting and corroborating times?

Common pitfalls that should not carry the burden
- Community/partner tokens integrated with the product but not issued or claimed by Polymarket as its own.
- “IOU,” voucher, or points/XP programs, whether or not represented in a database or tokenized wrapper.
- Testnet or canary deployments; contracts deployed without public availability; or listings on aggregators without Polymarket attribution.
- Market share/conditional outcome tokens used within markets, or base/settlement assets (e.g., stablecoins) supported by the platform.

Proof that would satisfy the proposition
- A dated, official Polymarket communication before the bar explicitly introducing the token as Polymarket’s and indicating launch/availability, ideally naming the contract/ticker.
- On‑chain provenance tying the token’s deployer/admin/treasury to Polymarket‑controlled addresses or a previously attested multisig, consistent with past custody patterns.
- Product, legal, or documentation pages on official domains describing the asset as Polymarket’s token.
- Independent temporal corroboration: archival captures and on‑chain block timestamps aligned and converted to PST.

Proof that would defeat the proposition
- Clear official denials or disclaimers that no token had launched by the deadline.
- Classification of any cited asset as an excluded category (NFT, promo, points/XP) or as a partner/community token.
- Absence of any official Polymarket announcement identifying a launched token before the bar, or announcements falling after the bar when times are converted to PST.
- On‑chain evidence showing the deployer/owner is not controlled by Polymarket.

Forward‑looking evidentiary plan
- Authenticate the set of Polymarket’s official channels for the period; collect and time‑fix all posts/pages referencing a token; preserve with archival captures.
- Inventory all candidate tokens and their contract addresses; analyze code, admin rights, and deployer custodianship; compare to known Polymarket custody patterns.
- Distinguish points/XP, NFTs, vouchers, and partner/community tokens from any purported “official” token.
- Rigorously convert and reconcile timestamps to PST and require congruence between on‑chain events and official communications.

Requested finding
If the proponent cannot produce an unambiguous, pre‑deadline official announcement paired with on‑chain provenance linking the token to Polymarket control, the proposition fails under a preponderance standard. Our position is that, on a completed record applying these criteria, the Council should find that no official Polymarket fungible token was launched and officially communicated before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST.

payload.text:
Council, the question is narrow and time‑barred: before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, did Polymarket launch an official fungible token and publicly communicate that launch via its own channels? On the present record, the answer is yes. Polymarket launched pUSD (Polymarket USD), a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, and announced and documented it on its official docs site in April 2026.

Element‑by‑element showing
1) Fungibility and transferability (met)
• Polymarket’s own pUSD concept page describes pUSD as “the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable, with 6 decimals, and backed by USDC. That is a user‑transferable fungible token, not an NFT, not points, and not a voucher. See PX‑2 (Item 1); PX‑1 (pUSD concept capture).

2) Official launch by Polymarket (met)
• Polymarket’s Contracts page states it is the “single source of truth” for addresses used across the platform and there lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB on Polygon mainnet (Chain ID 137). By placing pUSD on its official contracts roster and tying it to a specific address, Polymarket held pUSD out as its own token and assumed responsibility for it. See PX‑2 (Item 2); PX‑1 (Contracts capture).
• A Polygonscan page titled “Polymarket: pUSD Token” at that same address corroborates on‑chain existence and public use; attribution is established by Polymarket’s own Contracts page. See PX‑2 (Item 5); PX‑1.

3) Official communication (met)
• Polymarket’s changelog and migration guide—both on docs.polymarket.com—publicly communicate the launch: the Apr 17, 2026 entry announces a coordinated upgrade “including a new collateral token (pUSD)” with go‑live targeted for Apr 28, 2026, and the Apr 28, 2026 entry states the new exchange (CLOB V2) is live and points to the pUSD migration. The V2 migration guide likewise says Polymarket shipped “a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)” and explains its properties. See PX‑2 (Items 3–4); PX‑1 (Changelog and V2 migration captures).
• These are Polymarket‑controlled, official channels. They identify the token by name, brand it “Polymarket USD,” and communicate availability and use.

4) Timing (met)
• The changelog fixes go‑live at “Apr 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC,” and confirms “CLOB V2 is live” on Apr 28, 2026. 11:00 UTC on Apr 28, 2026 converts to 04:00 Pacific Time (UTC‑7) on Apr 28, 2026—more than eight months before the deadline of 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST. See PX‑2 (Item 3) and PX‑1.

Exclusions do not apply
• Not an NFT: pUSD is expressly a standard ERC‑20 fungible token. PX‑2 (Item 1).
• Not points/XP or a promo voucher: It is on‑chain, user‑transferable collateral for all trading. PX‑2 (Item 1).
• Not a partner/community token: It appears on Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” Contracts page as a Polymarket contract, under the Polymarket brand “pUSD.” PX‑2 (Item 2).

Addressing the defense’s likely theories
• “It’s only a collateral token.” The proposition does not exclude collateral tokens. It asks whether Polymarket launched “an official fungible token.” Polymarket branded and launched an ERC‑20 named Polymarket USD, documented it as its own, and put it into production; that satisfies the definition.
• “Supporting ≠ launching.” Here, Polymarket did more than support a third‑party asset: its Contracts page lists pUSD as a Polymarket contract and the docs call it Polymarket USD. That is official issuance/launch by Polymarket, not mere support for an external token.
• “No official announcement.” The changelog and migration guide are explicit, dated, and on official channels. They identify the token, state the launch, and link to the concept/contracts pages. That is sufficient official communication.

Standard of proof
• Under a preponderance standard, Polymarket’s own dated documents and contracts registry make it more likely than not that: (i) pUSD is a Polymarket‑launched ERC‑20; (ii) Polymarket publicly communicated its launch on Apr 17 and Apr 28, 2026; and (iii) the launch and communication occurred well before Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST.

Requested finding
Find for the plaintiff: Before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via its official channels. pUSD satisfies every element.

Exhibits
PX‑1: official‑source‑record.txt (manifest of captured Polymarket docs pages and Polygonscan corroboration).
PX‑2: primary‑evidence.txt (extracted material facts from the pUSD concept page, Contracts page, changelog, V2 migration guide, and Polygonscan).

1. Official Polymarket pUSD concept page, captured at source-captures/polymarket-docs-pusd.html.
   Material facts: pUSD is described by Polymarket as the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket, a standard ERC-20 token on Polygon, backed by USDC, transferable, and with 6 decimals.

2. Official Polymarket Contracts page, captured at source-captures/polymarket-docs-contracts.html.
   Material facts: the page states it is the single source of truth for Polymarket contract addresses, lists all Polymarket contracts on Polygon mainnet, and identifies pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy) at 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB.

3. Official Polymarket Changelog, captured at source-captures/polymarket-docs-changelog.html.
   Material facts: Apr 17, 2026 entry announces a coordinated upgrade including a new collateral token, pUSD, with go-live planned for Apr 28, 2026 at ~11:00 UTC. Apr 28, 2026 entry says CLOB V2 is live on production and points to the migration guide for pUSD.

4. Official Polymarket V2 migration guide, captured at source-captures/polymarket-docs-v2-migration.html.
   Material facts: says Polymarket shipped new Exchange contracts, a rewritten CLOB backend, and a new collateral token, Polymarket USD or pUSD; says CLOB V2 is live as of Apr 28, 2026; says pUSD is a standard ERC-20 backed by USDC.

5. Polygonscan pUSD token page, captured at source-captures/polygonscan-pusd-token.html.
   Material facts: page title identifies the address as Polymarket: pUSD Token; extracted page shows many recent token transactions and contract/source metadata. This corroborates production-chain existence but is secondary to Polymarket's own Contracts page for official attribution.

No market URL or Polymarket market identifier was provided in the prompt. No broader factual research has been performed yet, by instruction-boundary policy for prediction-market arbitration work.

"Polymarket launched an official token before January 1st, 2027 (00:00 PST). The token must be fungible, officially released by Polymarket, and communicated publicly through official Polymarket channels. NFT launches, promotional offerings, tokenized point systems, or token launches in collaboration with other projects and communities do not count as official tokens launched by Polymarket here."

Time window:
- Deadline: January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST.
- Condition can be demonstrated before the deadline if a qualifying token launch has already occurred.
- A negative final determination is premature before the deadline passes unless the arbitration is framed only as an as-of-record-date inquiry.

Resolution-condition elements preserved from the prompt:
1. Token must be fungible.
2. Token must be officially released by Polymarket.
3. Release must be communicated publicly through official Polymarket channels.
4. Exclusions: NFT launches, promotional offerings, tokenized point systems, and token launches in collaboration with other projects or communities.

2026-05-03 setup

Observe: the requester supplied a new Polymarket token-launch resolution condition and asked to run one arbitration. The skill guardrails require an explicit choice between open-record/search-enabled and closed-record/packet-only before starting a run. They also require asking before preliminary factual research.

payload.text:
Council,

Under a preponderance standard, the proponent must show it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST (08:00 UTC), Polymarket (i) launched a fungible token that it officially released as its own, and (ii) publicly communicated that launch through official channels, with timing fixed to the window and exclusions not triggered. On this record, that burden is not met.

Core problem with the proof posture
The proponent’s case rests almost entirely on two counsel‑written summaries (PX‑1, PX‑2). The actual primary materials they reference (the Polymarket docs pages and the Polygonscan page) are not in the record; only excerpts and characterizations are. PX‑1 itself concedes “Remaining official‑source limitations”: the captures were made after arbitration; there are no server publication timestamps; no archive snapshots; and the explorer evidence is merely corroborative. Those concessions go to authenticity, attribution, and, above all, timing—the very elements the proposition turns on. With those gaps, the Council cannot fix what was said by Polymarket, when, and in what exact language.

Element‑by‑element rebuttal
1) Fungibility and transferability (not proven)
PX‑2 reports that a “pUSD” is a standard ERC‑20, transferable, 6 decimals, backed by USDC. But there is no contract code, ABI, or transfer‑restriction analysis in the record; no on‑chain event timeline; no proof that transfers are open and user‑controlled as opposed to protocol‑restricted. A counsel summary of an absent web page does not carry the preponderance standard on this element.

2) Official launch by Polymarket (not proven)
The theory that listing “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” on a “Contracts” docs page shows Polymarket launched the token assumes what it needs to prove. The record provides no deployer/admin provenance tying the cited address to a Polymarket‑controlled multisig or previously attested custody. Platforms routinely document integrated assets; documentation is not the same as issuer control. Without on‑chain provenance or a primary Polymarket attestation that it issued and is responsible for the token’s contract, “official launch by Polymarket” remains unproven.

3) Official communication of the launch (not proven)
The proposition requires public communication by Polymarket of the token’s launch, not merely internal component documentation. PX‑1/PX‑2 quote a “changelog” and a “V2 migration guide,” but those primary pages are not in evidence with headers, timestamps, or archive captures. The quoted language itself is equivocal on timing: an April 17, 2026 entry “targets” a go‑live; an April 28 entry says “CLOB V2 is live” and “points to the migration,” which speaks to an exchange upgrade, not an unequivocal token launch announcement. No official blog post, press page, or in‑app/verified social announcement introducing “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as launched appears in the record.

4) Timing to the bar (not proven)
Even if one credited the quoted docs, PX‑1 acknowledges there are no server timestamps or archive snapshots. There is no block‑time evidence for contract creation/mint, no first‑use transactions, and no PST‑converted timeline tied to primary artifacts. Without that, the Council cannot reliably place both launch and official communication before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST.

Exclusions and classification concerns (unresolved on this record)
PX‑2 characterizes pUSD as a collateral token “backed by USDC.” That description is consistent with an internal accounting/custody wrapper used to settle trades, which the market’s exclusions caution against (tokenized point/balance systems or similar non‑public assets). If transfers are permissioned or limited to protocol contracts, it would not satisfy the user‑transferable fungible‑asset element. The proponent offers no code, policy page, or independent analysis to dispel those possibilities.

Weight of the evidence
The only concrete on‑chain reference in the record is a token address recited in PX‑1/PX‑2, paired with a claim that an explorer page’s title reads “Polymarket: pUSD Token.” Explorer labels are editable and not authoritative for issuer attribution; and, again, the underlying page is not in evidence with a timestamp. Under preponderance, unverified summaries with explicit authenticity/timing caveats do not outweigh the absence of primary, time‑fixed proof.

What would satisfy the proposition (and is missing)
- Primary, dated Polymarket communications (docs/blog/announcements or in‑app notices) stating that Polymarket launched “Polymarket USD (pUSD),” preserved with archive timestamps predating the bar.
- On‑chain provenance that the pUSD contract was deployed/administered by a Polymarket‑controlled address, with block‑time timestamps converted to PST.
- Technical confirmation of open ERC‑20 transferability (no protocol‑only transfer restrictions) and that the asset is not merely an internal points/balance wrapper.

Requested finding
On this closed record, the proponent has not shown—by a preponderance of the evidence—that before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via its own channels. Find for the defendant.

technical_reports.summary:
We directly checked Polymarket’s official documentation and PolygonScan. Findings:

• Changelog (official docs): Contains entries titled “Apr 17, 2026 — CLOB V2: upgrades go live April 28 at ~11:00 UTC … and a new collateral token (pUSD)” and “Apr 28, 2026 — CLOB V2 is live … Migration guide: See Migrating to CLOB V2.” This is official, dated communication identifying pUSD and the go‑live. [docs.polymarket.com/changelog] (See web source turn4view0, lines 139–160.)

• Migration guide (official docs): States “Polymarket has shipped … a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD). CLOB V2 is live as of April 28, 2026.” [docs.polymarket.com/v2-migration] (turn12view0 lines 140–154.)

• pUSD concept page (official docs): “pUSD (Polymarket USD) … It’s a standard ERC‑20 token on Polygon … Transferable — Yes … Decimals — 6.” [docs.polymarket.com/concepts/pusd] (turn5view0 lines 120–142.)

• Contracts page (official docs): States “single source of truth” for addresses and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB, plus onramp/offramp. [docs.polymarket.com/resources/contracts] (turn7view0 lines 125–147.)

• PolygonScan (corroboration): Address 0xC011…E82DFB page title shows “Polymarket: pUSD Token,” and “Contract Creator: Polymarket: Deployer 1.” [polygonscan.com/address/0xC011…E82DFB] (turn8view0 lines 125–215.)

• Timing conversion: Apr 28, 2026 11:00 UTC = Apr 28, 2026 04:00 Pacific (UTC−7). Both launch and official communications precede 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST by months.

Together, these primary materials confirm: (1) pUSD is a standard, transferable ERC‑20; (2) Polymarket officially attributed pUSD to itself and published the contract address; and (3) Polymarket publicly communicated the launch and go‑live within April 2026.
technical_reports.title: Targeted primary‑source validation: pUSD launch, attribution, and timing

payload.text:
Council, the defense’s objections reduce to (i) “no primary sources/timestamps,” (ii) “no issuer control,” and (iii) “non‑transferable/points.” Each fails on this record; and to remove any doubt, we file a short primary‑source validation as a technical report.

1) Official communication is in the record — from Polymarket itself
• PX‑1/PX‑2 quote Polymarket’s Changelog and V2 Migration Guide, which are Polymarket‑controlled channels and expressly identify “a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD),” set the Apr 28, 2026 cutover, and then state “CLOB V2 is live,” pointing readers to the pUSD migration. Those pages are party admissions and product communications. The attached technical report captures those very pages as they read today and confirms the quoted language and dates.

2) Issuer/control is established by Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” roster
• PX‑1/PX‑2 point to the Contracts page stating it is “the single source of truth for all contract addresses used across the platform,” which lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011…E82DFB. That is a first‑party attestation tying a specific ERC‑20 to Polymarket. Under a preponderance standard, the operator’s own contracts roster is stronger attribution than any third‑party label. The technical report also notes Polygonscan’s page for that address shows “Polymarket: pUSD Token” and a creator labeled “Polymarket: Deployer 1,” consistent with issuer control.

3) Fungibility/transferability is answered by Polymarket’s own concept page
• PX‑1/PX‑2 quote the Polymarket USD (pUSD) concept page: pUSD is a “standard ERC‑20 token on Polygon,” with 6 decimals and “Transferable — Yes.” That is the opposite of points/XP or a non‑transferable balance wrapper. No NFT, no promo, no partner/community token.

4) Timing to the bar is pinned by Polymarket’s dated notices
• The Apr 17, 2026 changelog entry fixes go‑live at “~11:00 UTC” on Apr 28, 2026, and the Apr 28, 2026 entry confirms “CLOB V2 is live” and directs users to the pUSD migration. April 28, 2026 at 11:00 UTC converts to 04:00 Pacific Time (UTC−7) on April 28, 2026 — over eight months before the Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline. The technical report reproduces these entries from Polymarket’s own site.

Weight of evidence
• The defense offers no contrary primary evidence. By contrast, the plaintiff’s proof consists of Polymarket’s own documentation naming and describing “Polymarket USD (pUSD),” the first‑party contracts roster tying pUSD to a specific address, and dated changelog/migration entries announcing and confirming go‑live. That comfortably clears preponderance on all elements and outside the exclusions.

Requested finding
Find for the plaintiff: Before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via its official channels. pUSD satisfies every element.

Council,

The rebuttal purports to cure three defects with a “technical report” that repeats the same docs pages and adds an explorer label. On this closed record, those defects remain and preponderance is still not met.

1) Timing/authentication gap persists
• PX‑1 itself concedes the key limitation: the captures were made after the run and there are no server publication timestamps or archive snapshots. That was the core problem we identified, and nothing in the rebuttal changes it. The “technical report” cites what the pages read today and references internal view ids, but it does not add independent, time‑fixed evidence (e.g., Memento/Wayback, server‑signed headers, repo commits) showing those statements were publicly communicated on April 17/28, 2026 rather than later edits. Without time‑fixing, the Council cannot reliably place the alleged “official communication” before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST. The UTC→PST conversion is undisputed; what’s missing is proof that those entries existed on those dates.

2) “Official launch by Polymarket” is still not established
• The Contracts page excerpt in PX‑1/PX‑2 describes a “single source of truth for addresses used across the platform” and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy).” That is evidence of integration and address discovery, not issuer provenance or launch. There is no owner/admin mapping to a Polymarket‑controlled multisig, no deployer analysis, and no primary Polymarket statement that it issued the token. The rebuttal leans on a “Polygonscan: Contract Creator — Polymarket: Deployer 1” label cited only in the technical report narrative; explorer labels are editable and, without on‑chain key mapping in evidence, cannot carry issuer control under a preponderance standard.

3) Fungibility/transferability and exclusions remain unresolved
• The concept‑page bullet “Transferable — Yes” is a product claim, not technical proof. The record lacks code, policy, or transaction analysis showing open, user‑initiated EOA↔EOA transfers without protocol permissioning. PX‑1’s mention of “many recent token transactions” does not distinguish contract churn from free user transfers. If pUSD functions as an internal settlement wrapper “backed by USDC,” it risks falling within the spirit of excluded tokenized balance/points systems or, at minimum, fails the element requiring a user‑transferable fungible asset proven on this record.

4) “Official communication of the launch” is still ambiguous
• Even crediting the quoted docs, the April 17 entry “targets” a go‑live and the April 28 entry says “CLOB V2 is live” and points to migration. That language documents a system upgrade; it is not the same as a dated, unambiguous announcement that “Polymarket launched its token (Polymarket USD, pUSD) today,” and the record provides no contemporaneous blog, press, in‑app notice, or social post doing so. On a narrow proposition that requires public communication of a token launch, that ambiguity matters.

Bottom line
The plaintiff’s best evidence remains (a) mutable docs pages without independent, pre‑deadline time‑fixing and (b) a contracts roster proving integration, not issuance. Against that backdrop, the elements of (i) official launch by Polymarket and (ii) timely, official communication are not shown to be more likely than not. Find for the defendant.

Council,

The rebuttal purports to cure three defects with a “technical report” that repeats the same docs pages and adds an explorer label. On this closed record, those defects remain and preponderance is still not met.

1) Timing/authentication gap persists
• PX‑1 itself concedes the key limitation: the captures were made after the run and there are no server publication timestamps or archive snapshots. That was the core problem we identified, and nothing in the rebuttal changes it. The “technical report” cites what the pages read today and references internal view ids, but it does not add independent, time‑fixed evidence (e.g., Memento/Wayback, server‑signed headers, repo commits) showing those statements were publicly communicated on April 17/28, 2026 rather than later edits. Without time‑fixing, the Council cannot reliably place the alleged “official communication” before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST. The UTC→PST conversion is undisputed; what’s missing is proof that those entries existed on those dates.

2) “Official launch by Polymarket” is still not established
• The Contracts page excerpt in PX‑1/PX‑2 describes a “single source of truth for addresses used across the platform” and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy).” That is evidence of integration and address discovery, not issuer provenance or launch. There is no owner/admin mapping to a Polymarket‑controlled multisig, no deployer analysis, and no primary Polymarket statement that it issued the token. The rebuttal leans on a “Polygonscan: Contract Creator — Polymarket: Deployer 1” label cited only in the technical report narrative; explorer labels are editable and, without on‑chain key mapping in evidence, cannot carry issuer control under a preponderance standard.

3) Fungibility/transferability and exclusions remain unresolved
• The concept‑page bullet “Transferable — Yes” is a product claim, not technical proof. The record lacks code, policy, or transaction analysis showing open, user‑initiated EOA↔EOA transfers without protocol permissioning. PX‑1’s mention of “many recent token transactions” does not distinguish contract churn from free user transfers. If pUSD functions as an internal settlement wrapper “backed by USDC,” it risks falling within the spirit of excluded tokenized balance/points systems or, at minimum, fails the element requiring a user‑transferable fungible asset proven on this record.

4) “Official communication of the launch” is still ambiguous
• Even crediting the quoted docs, the April 17 entry “targets” a go‑live and the April 28 entry says “CLOB V2 is live” and points to migration. That language documents a system upgrade; it is not the same as a dated, unambiguous announcement that “Polymarket launched its token (Polymarket USD, pUSD) today,” and the record provides no contemporaneous blog, press, in‑app notice, or social post doing so. On a narrow proposition that requires public communication of a token launch, that ambiguity matters.

Bottom line
The plaintiff’s best evidence remains (a) mutable docs pages without independent, pre‑deadline time‑fixing and (b) a contracts roster proving integration, not issuance. Against that backdrop, the elements of (i) official launch by Polymarket and (ii) timely, official communication are not shown to be more likely than not. Find for the defendant.

Find for the plaintiff on the proposition.

arguments.text:
Council, the question is narrow and time‑barred: before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, did Polymarket launch an official fungible token and publicly communicate that launch via its own channels? On the present record, the answer is yes. Polymarket launched pUSD (Polymarket USD), a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, and announced and documented it on its official docs site in April 2026.

Element‑by‑element showing
1) Fungibility and transferability (met)
• Polymarket’s own pUSD concept page describes pUSD as “the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable, with 6 decimals, and backed by USDC. That is a user‑transferable fungible token, not an NFT, not points, and not a voucher. See PX‑2 (Item 1); PX‑1 (pUSD concept capture).

2) Official launch by Polymarket (met)
• Polymarket’s Contracts page states it is the “single source of truth” for addresses used across the platform and there lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB on Polygon mainnet (Chain ID 137). By placing pUSD on its official contracts roster and tying it to a specific address, Polymarket held pUSD out as its own token and assumed responsibility for it. See PX‑2 (Item 2); PX‑1 (Contracts capture).
• A Polygonscan page titled “Polymarket: pUSD Token” at that same address corroborates on‑chain existence and public use; attribution is established by Polymarket’s own Contracts page. See PX‑2 (Item 5); PX‑1.

3) Official communication (met)
• Polymarket’s changelog and migration guide—both on docs.polymarket.com—publicly communicate the launch: the Apr 17, 2026 entry announces a coordinated upgrade “including a new collateral token (pUSD)” with go‑live targeted for Apr 28, 2026, and the Apr 28, 2026 entry states the new exchange (CLOB V2) is live and points to the pUSD migration. The V2 migration guide likewise says Polymarket shipped “a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)” and explains its properties. See PX‑2 (Items 3–4); PX‑1 (Changelog and V2 migration captures).
• These are Polymarket‑controlled, official channels. They identify the token by name, brand it “Polymarket USD,” and communicate availability and use.

4) Timing (met)
• The changelog fixes go‑live at “Apr 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC,” and confirms “CLOB V2 is live” on Apr 28, 2026. 11:00 UTC on Apr 28, 2026 converts to 04:00 Pacific Time (UTC‑7) on Apr 28, 2026—more than eight months before the deadline of 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST. See PX‑2 (Item 3) and PX‑1.

Exclusions do not apply
• Not an NFT: pUSD is expressly a standard ERC‑20 fungible token. PX‑2 (Item 1).
• Not points/XP or a promo voucher: It is on‑chain, user‑transferable collateral for all trading. PX‑2 (Item 1).
• Not a partner/community token: It appears on Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” Contracts page as a Polymarket contract, under the Polymarket brand “pUSD.” PX‑2 (Item 2).

Addressing the defense’s likely theories
• “It’s only a collateral token.” The proposition does not exclude collateral tokens. It asks whether Polymarket launched “an official fungible token.” Polymarket branded and launched an ERC‑20 named Polymarket USD, documented it as its own, and put it into production; that satisfies the definition.
• “Supporting ≠ launching.” Here, Polymarket did more than support a third‑party asset: its Contracts page lists pUSD as a Polymarket contract and the docs call it Polymarket USD. That is official issuance/launch by Polymarket, not mere support for an external token.
• “No official announcement.” The changelog and migration guide are explicit, dated, and on official channels. They identify the token, state the launch, and link to the concept/contracts pages. That is sufficient official communication.

Standard of proof
• Under a preponderance standard, Polymarket’s own dated documents and contracts registry make it more likely than not that: (i) pUSD is a Polymarket‑launched ERC‑20; (ii) Polymarket publicly communicated its launch on Apr 17 and Apr 28, 2026; and (iii) the launch and communication occurred well before Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST.

Requested finding
Find for the plaintiff: Before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via its official channels. pUSD satisfies every element.

Exhibits
PX‑1: official‑source‑record.txt (manifest of captured Polymarket docs pages and Polygonscan corroboration).
PX‑2: primary‑evidence.txt (extracted material facts from the pUSD concept page, Contracts page, changelog, V2 migration guide, and Polygonscan).

Council,

Under a preponderance standard, the proponent must show it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST (08:00 UTC), Polymarket (i) launched a fungible token that it officially released as its own, and (ii) publicly communicated that launch through official channels, with timing fixed to the window and exclusions not triggered. On this record, that burden is not met.

Core problem with the proof posture
The proponent’s case rests almost entirely on two counsel‑written summaries (PX‑1, PX‑2). The actual primary materials they reference (the Polymarket docs pages and the Polygonscan page) are not in the record; only excerpts and characterizations are. PX‑1 itself concedes “Remaining official‑source limitations”: the captures were made after arbitration; there are no server publication timestamps; no archive snapshots; and the explorer evidence is merely corroborative. Those concessions go to authenticity, attribution, and, above all, timing—the very elements the proposition turns on. With those gaps, the Council cannot fix what was said by Polymarket, when, and in what exact language.

Element‑by‑element rebuttal
1) Fungibility and transferability (not proven)
PX‑2 reports that a “pUSD” is a standard ERC‑20, transferable, 6 decimals, backed by USDC. But there is no contract code, ABI, or transfer‑restriction analysis in the record; no on‑chain event timeline; no proof that transfers are open and user‑controlled as opposed to protocol‑restricted. A counsel summary of an absent web page does not carry the preponderance standard on this element.

2) Official launch by Polymarket (not proven)
The theory that listing “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” on a “Contracts” docs page shows Polymarket launched the token assumes what it needs to prove. The record provides no deployer/admin provenance tying the cited address to a Polymarket‑controlled multisig or previously attested custody. Platforms routinely document integrated assets; documentation is not the same as issuer control. Without on‑chain provenance or a primary Polymarket attestation that it issued and is responsible for the token’s contract, “official launch by Polymarket” remains unproven.

3) Official communication of the launch (not proven)
The proposition requires public communication by Polymarket of the token’s launch, not merely internal component documentation. PX‑1/PX‑2 quote a “changelog” and a “V2 migration guide,” but those primary pages are not in evidence with headers, timestamps, or archive captures. The quoted language itself is equivocal on timing: an April 17, 2026 entry “targets” a go‑live; an April 28 entry says “CLOB V2 is live” and “points to the migration,” which speaks to an exchange upgrade, not an unequivocal token launch announcement. No official blog post, press page, or in‑app/verified social announcement introducing “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as launched appears in the record.

4) Timing to the bar (not proven)
Even if one credited the quoted docs, PX‑1 acknowledges there are no server timestamps or archive snapshots. There is no block‑time evidence for contract creation/mint, no first‑use transactions, and no PST‑converted timeline tied to primary artifacts. Without that, the Council cannot reliably place both launch and official communication before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST.

Exclusions and classification concerns (unresolved on this record)
PX‑2 characterizes pUSD as a collateral token “backed by USDC.” That description is consistent with an internal accounting/custody wrapper used to settle trades, which the market’s exclusions caution against (tokenized point/balance systems or similar non‑public assets). If transfers are permissioned or limited to protocol contracts, it would not satisfy the user‑transferable fungible‑asset element. The proponent offers no code, policy page, or independent analysis to dispel those possibilities.

Weight of the evidence
The only concrete on‑chain reference in the record is a token address recited in PX‑1/PX‑2, paired with a claim that an explorer page’s title reads “Polymarket: pUSD Token.” Explorer labels are editable and not authoritative for issuer attribution; and, again, the underlying page is not in evidence with a timestamp. Under preponderance, unverified summaries with explicit authenticity/timing caveats do not outweigh the absence of primary, time‑fixed proof.

What would satisfy the proposition (and is missing)
- Primary, dated Polymarket communications (docs/blog/announcements or in‑app notices) stating that Polymarket launched “Polymarket USD (pUSD),” preserved with archive timestamps predating the bar.
- On‑chain provenance that the pUSD contract was deployed/administered by a Polymarket‑controlled address, with block‑time timestamps converted to PST.
- Technical confirmation of open ERC‑20 transferability (no protocol‑only transfer restrictions) and that the asset is not merely an internal points/balance wrapper.

Requested finding
On this closed record, the proponent has not shown—by a preponderance of the evidence—that before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via its own channels. Find for the defendant.

case.caption: Claimant v. Respondent

closings.text:
Council,

Question: Before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, did Polymarket launch an official fungible token and publicly communicate that launch via its own channels? Under a preponderance standard, the record shows yes.

Proven elements
1) A fungible, user‑transferable token
• Polymarket’s pUSD concept page describes “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, explicitly “Transferable — Yes,” with 6 decimals and backed by USDC. That is a fungible ERC‑20, not an NFT, voucher, or points/XP wrapper. (PX‑1; PX‑2 ¶1.)

2) Official attribution/launch by Polymarket
• Polymarket’s Contracts page states it is the “single source of truth for all contract addresses used across the platform” and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011…E82DFB on Polygon mainnet. Publishing pUSD on this first‑party roster is Polymarket holding the token out as its own official asset and assuming responsibility for the address. (PX‑1; PX‑2 ¶2.)
• Polygonscan for the same address is titled “Polymarket: pUSD Token,” with creator “Polymarket: Deployer 1.” This is corroborative and aligns with Polymarket’s own attribution. (PX‑1; PX‑2 ¶5; Technical Report.)

3) Official communication and timing
• Polymarket’s changelog (docs.polymarket.com) announces on April 17, 2026 a coordinated upgrade “including a new collateral token (pUSD)” with go‑live targeted for April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC; the April 28, 2026 entry states “CLOB V2 is live” and directs users to the pUSD migration. (PX‑1; PX‑2 ¶3.)
• The V2 migration guide states Polymarket “has shipped … a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)” and confirms CLOB V2 is live as of April 28, 2026. (PX‑1; PX‑2 ¶4.)
• 11:00 UTC April 28, 2026 = 04:00 Pacific (UTC−7) April 28, 2026—well before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST bar. (PX‑1; PX‑2 ¶3.)

Why this satisfies the proposition
• “Official fungible token”: Named “Polymarket USD,” specified as a standard, transferable ERC‑20; listed on Polymarket’s own Contracts roster as the single source of truth. That is official and fungible.
• “Launched”: The upgrade Polymarket announced and then declared live includes “a new collateral token (pUSD).” When the upgrade went live, so did pUSD.
• “Officially communicated”: The changelog and migration guide are Polymarket‑controlled, dated pages identifying the token and its live cutover. That suffices.
• “Timing”: The entries fix go‑live to April 28, 2026, months before the deadline.

Addressing the defense’s strongest objections
1) “No time‑fixed archives/server stamps.” Preponderance does not demand archival proofs; it weighs what is more likely than not. Here we have Polymarket’s own dated communications and its first‑party contracts roster. The defense offers no contrary primary evidence—only speculation that pages could have been edited. On balance, the operator’s contemporaneously dated statements are more persuasive.

2) “Integration ≠ issuance.” This is not a generic integration list; it is Polymarket’s “single source of truth” for its contracts, listing a branded token (“pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)”) at a specific address. Coupled with the docs naming “Polymarket USD,” that is first‑party attribution amounting to launch and responsibility. Explorer metadata (“Polymarket: Deployer 1”) corroborates.

3) “Non‑transferable/points risk.” The concept page squarely states “Transferable — Yes,” “standard ERC‑20,” and positions pUSD as on‑chain collateral. That is the opposite of points/XP or a non‑transferable internal balance. No exclusion applies.

4) “Upgrade notice isn’t a token launch.” The required official communication is present and explicit that the upgrade includes “a new collateral token (pUSD),” followed by “CLOB V2 is live.” A launch can occur as part of a production cutover; no standalone “token launch” blog headline is required.

Bottom line
Polymarket’s own documentation identifies, brands, and attributes pUSD; sets its production cutover to April 28, 2026; and points users to a migration. Its Contracts page ties pUSD to a specific address as the single source of truth. Corroborating explorer data aligns. Under preponderance, that is sufficient to find that before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through its official channels.

Requested finding
Find for the plaintiff on the proposition.

Council,

Under a preponderance standard, the plaintiff must prove it is more likely than not that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched an official fungible token and (ii) publicly communicated that launch via its own channels. On this record, key elements remain unproven.

Decisive gaps
1) Timing and authentication are not established. The plaintiff’s own exhibit (PX‑1) concedes the core limitation: the cited Polymarket docs pages were captured after the run, without server publication timestamps or archive snapshots. That means the record does not fix what those pages said on April 17 or April 28, 2026—or that those entries existed on those dates. The rebuttal’s “technical report” repeats the same pages as they read now; it does not supply independent, time‑fixed proof. Without time‑fixing, the Council cannot reliably place the alleged official communication before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST bar.

2) Issuer control and “official launch by Polymarket” are not proven. The Contracts page excerpt (PX‑1/PX‑2) describes a “single source of truth for addresses used across the platform” and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy).” That is an integration/roster function; it does not itself prove Polymarket issued the token or controls its admin keys. The record contains no deployer/admin provenance mapping the token to a Polymarket‑controlled multisig or previously attested release address. The plaintiff leans on an explorer label (“Polymarket: Deployer 1”) referenced in argument; explorer metadata is editable and, without on‑chain key mapping in evidence, cannot carry issuer attribution under preponderance.

3) Fungibility/transferability and exclusions remain unresolved. The concept page language quoted in PX‑1/PX‑2 says “standard ERC‑20” and “Transferable — Yes,” but there is no contract code, ABI, or transaction analysis in the record demonstrating open EOA↔EOA transfers rather than protocol‑permissioned movement. The same pages characterize pUSD as collateral “backed by USDC,” consistent with an internal settlement wrapper. On this record, the possibility that pUSD functions like an internal balance/points‑style wrapper has not been dispelled, and exclusions caution against counting such systems. Unresolved material questions cut against the proponent’s burden.

4) Official communication of a token launch is ambiguous. Even accepting the quoted text at face value, the April 17 entry merely “targets” a go‑live and the April 28 entry states “CLOB V2 is live” and points to a migration guide. That is a system upgrade notice. The record contains no unambiguous, dated Polymarket announcement that it launched “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as a token on that date. On a proposition that requires public communication of the token’s launch, ambiguity matters—especially given the timing/authentication gap above.

Addressing the plaintiff’s best points
- “Single source of truth” + named pUSD + address: A first‑party address roster shows what the platform integrates; it is not the same as proof of issuance/launch responsibility. Without deployer/admin mapping, control by Polymarket is not more likely than not.
- Dated changelog/migration entries: The plaintiff’s own exhibit acknowledges the absence of server timestamps and archival captures. With no time‑fixed evidence, the Council cannot confidently place those communications before the bar.
- Explorer corroboration: Labels on a block explorer are not authoritative issuer attestation in this record and do not cure the missing on‑chain provenance.

Bottom line
To satisfy the proposition, the plaintiff needed time‑fixed official communications and on‑chain provenance tying the token to Polymarket control, plus reliable proof of open, user‑transferable fungibility outside the exclusions—all before January 1, 2027 00:00 PST. Instead, the record offers mutable docs pages without independent timestamps, an integration roster without issuer provenance, and ambiguous upgrade language. Under a preponderance standard, those gaps mean the elements are not more likely than not satisfied.

Requested finding
Find for the defendant: the proponent has not carried its burden that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via its own channels.

openings.text:
Council, the proposition is narrow and time‑barred: Before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, did Polymarket launch an official fungible token and publicly communicate that launch via its own channels? NFTs, promos, tokenized points, and co‑branded/community tokens do not count.

Theory
Polymarket did launch such a token within the window and acknowledged it through official communications. The decisive questions are who launched what, how it was described by Polymarket itself, and when.

Elements the proponent must prove (all required)
1) Fungibility and transferability: An on‑chain, user‑transferable fungible asset (e.g., ERC‑20‑type), not an NFT, points, or internal balance.
2) Official launch by Polymarket: The issuer/launcher is Polymarket (as brand/operator) or its authorized deployer under its control.
3) Official communication: Polymarket publicly communicated the token’s launch through channels it controls (primary site, official blog, verified social/account feeds, product or policy pages). Third‑party press is insufficient standing alone.
4) Timing: Both launch and official communication occurred on or before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST (08:00 UTC). Ambiguous timestamps must be resolved to this bar.

What does not satisfy the proposition
- NFTs, loyalty/XP/points (even if “tokenized” but not openly transferable), promo vouchers, or speculative placeholders.
- Third‑party/partner/community tokens, even if integrated on the platform, without Polymarket holding them out as its own official token.

Disputed issues framed for decision
- Identity: Is there a specific fungible token Polymarket held out as its own official asset (name/ticker/contract)?
- Control/issuance: Do deployer/multisig/signers or commissioning records tie the contract to Polymarket authority?
- Official speech: Did Polymarket’s own channels describe or announce the token as launched?
- Temporal placement: Do reliable, dated artifacts place the launch and the official communication before the PST deadline?
- Exclusion fit: Does the asset fall into any excluded category or a co‑launch not attributable to Polymarket?

Proof that would satisfy preponderance
- Dated, official Polymarket announcement identifying the token as its own and stating or implying launch availability.
- On‑chain evidence linking the token’s deployer/owner to Polymarket (e.g., previously attested multisig, audited release addresses, or consistent corporate custody patterns).
- Product/legal/policy materials on official domains characterizing the asset as Polymarket’s token.
- Archival captures confirming the content and dates of official pages/posts; on‑chain timestamping corroborating deployment/mint.

Proof that would defeat the proposition
- Clear, dated, official denials that Polymarket launched any token in time.
- Conclusive classification of any cited asset as NFT, points, promo, or a partner/community token.
- No trace of official Polymarket communication acknowledging a token launch before the deadline.
- Mismatch between claimed identifiers and any asset Polymarket publicly embraced as its own.

Forward‑looking evidentiary plan
- Authenticate Polymarket’s official channels and affiliated entities for the period.
- Collect dated announcements and product/policy pages referencing any token.
- Resolve contract provenance to Polymarket‑controlled keys or commissioned deployers.
- Fix all times to PST with archival and on‑chain records.
- Rigorously screen for exclusions and co‑branding.

Requested finding
On the completed record, find that Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, under a preponderance standard.

Council, the proposition is narrow and time‑barred: before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, did Polymarket launch an “official fungible token” and publicly communicate that launch via its own channels? Under a preponderance standard, the proponent must show it is more likely than not that every element is met. Our theory: Polymarket did not do so within the window. Any candidate the proponent advances will fall into an excluded category, lack official launch communication, lack issuer control by Polymarket, or miss the deadline.

Working definitions (to guide the decision)
- Official fungible token: A user‑transferable on‑chain fungible asset (ERC‑20‑type or equivalent) that Polymarket itself holds out as its own (by name/ticker/contract) and for which it accepts responsibility or control. Merely supporting third‑party assets is insufficient.
- Launch: Public availability, not teasers or “coming soon.” The contract must be live for users and identifiable.
- Official communication: A clear, dated message on Polymarket‑controlled channels (site, blog/docs, verified social, in‑app notices, or policy/product pages) that identifies the token as Polymarket’s and states or unmistakably implies launch/availability. Third‑party press/community posts are not enough.
- Exclusions: NFTs; promotional coupons/vouchers; point/XP/loyalty systems even if “tokenized”; and tokens launched in collaboration with other projects/communities where the token is not held out as Polymarket’s own.
- Timing: Both launch and qualifying official communication must occur on or before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST. Ambiguous timestamps and time‑zone conversions cannot carry the proponent’s burden.

Disputed questions likely to decide the case
1) Identity: Is there a specific fungible token Polymarket held out as its own—name/ticker and contract—before the deadline?
2) Issuer/control: Do on‑chain deployer/admin keys map to Polymarket‑controlled custody (e.g., attested multisigs/addresses), rather than a partner/community actor?
3) Official speech: Did Polymarket’s own channels explicitly announce or clearly characterize the token as launched within the window?
4) Transferability/availability: Was the asset user‑transferable on‑chain at launch, or merely internal points, claims, or a non‑transferable balance?
5) Temporal placement: Do verifiable records place both the on‑chain launch and the official communication before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, after conversion to PST?

Pitfalls that should not carry the burden
- Community/partner tokens integrated with the product but not issued or claimed by Polymarket as its own.
- IOUs/vouchers/points or XP programs (database or tokenized wrappers).
- Testnet/canary deployments; contracts without public availability; aggregator listings without Polymarket attribution.
- Market/outcome tokens used within markets, or base assets the platform supports.

Proof that would satisfy the proposition
- A dated, official Polymarket communication before the bar explicitly introducing the token as Polymarket’s and indicating launch/availability, ideally naming the contract/ticker.
- On‑chain provenance tying the token’s deployer/admin/treasury to Polymarket‑controlled addresses or a previously attested multisig.
- Product/legal/docs pages on official domains describing the asset as Polymarket’s token.
- Archival captures and on‑chain timestamps aligned and converted to PST.

Proof that would defeat the proposition
- Clear official denials or disclaimers that no token had launched by the deadline.
- A showing that any cited asset is excluded (NFT, promo, points/XP) or is a partner/community token.
- Absence of an official Polymarket announcement identifying a launched token before the bar, or announcements falling after the bar when converted to PST.
- On‑chain evidence that the deployer/owner is not controlled by Polymarket.

Forward‑looking evidentiary plan
- Authenticate Polymarket’s official channels for the period; collect and time‑fix all posts/pages referencing any token; preserve with archives.
- Inventory candidate tokens and contract addresses; analyze code, admin rights, and deployer custodianship; compare to known custody patterns.
- Distinguish points/XP, NFTs, vouchers, and partner/community tokens from any purported “official” token.
- Rigorously reconcile PST‑converted timestamps with on‑chain events and official communications.

Requested finding
On a completed record applying these criteria, if the proponent cannot pair a pre‑deadline official announcement with on‑chain provenance linking the token to Polymarket control, the proposition fails under a preponderance standard.

case.proposition: Before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token: a fungible token officially released by Polymarket and publicly communicated through official Polymarket channels, excluding NFT launches, promotional offerings, tokenized point systems, and token launches in collaboration with other projects or communities.

rebuttals.text:
Council, the defense’s objections reduce to (i) “no primary sources/timestamps,” (ii) “no issuer control,” and (iii) “non‑transferable/points.” Each fails on this record; and to remove any doubt, we file a short primary‑source validation as a technical report.

1) Official communication is in the record — from Polymarket itself
• PX‑1/PX‑2 quote Polymarket’s Changelog and V2 Migration Guide, which are Polymarket‑controlled channels and expressly identify “a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD),” set the Apr 28, 2026 cutover, and then state “CLOB V2 is live,” pointing readers to the pUSD migration. Those pages are party admissions and product communications. The attached technical report captures those very pages as they read today and confirms the quoted language and dates.

2) Issuer/control is established by Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” roster
• PX‑1/PX‑2 point to the Contracts page stating it is “the single source of truth for all contract addresses used across the platform,” which lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” at 0xC011…E82DFB. That is a first‑party attestation tying a specific ERC‑20 to Polymarket. Under a preponderance standard, the operator’s own contracts roster is stronger attribution than any third‑party label. The technical report also notes Polygonscan’s page for that address shows “Polymarket: pUSD Token” and a creator labeled “Polymarket: Deployer 1,” consistent with issuer control.

3) Fungibility/transferability is answered by Polymarket’s own concept page
• PX‑1/PX‑2 quote the Polymarket USD (pUSD) concept page: pUSD is a “standard ERC‑20 token on Polygon,” with 6 decimals and “Transferable — Yes.” That is the opposite of points/XP or a non‑transferable balance wrapper. No NFT, no promo, no partner/community token.

4) Timing to the bar is pinned by Polymarket’s dated notices
• The Apr 17, 2026 changelog entry fixes go‑live at “~11:00 UTC” on Apr 28, 2026, and the Apr 28, 2026 entry confirms “CLOB V2 is live” and directs users to the pUSD migration. April 28, 2026 at 11:00 UTC converts to 04:00 Pacific Time (UTC−7) on April 28, 2026 — over eight months before the Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline. The technical report reproduces these entries from Polymarket’s own site.

Weight of evidence
• The defense offers no contrary primary evidence. By contrast, the plaintiff’s proof consists of Polymarket’s own documentation naming and describing “Polymarket USD (pUSD),” the first‑party contracts roster tying pUSD to a specific address, and dated changelog/migration entries announcing and confirming go‑live. That comfortably clears preponderance on all elements and outside the exclusions.

Requested finding
Find for the plaintiff: Before 2027‑01‑01 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via its official channels. pUSD satisfies every element.

surrebuttals.text:
Council,

The rebuttal purports to cure three defects with a “technical report” that repeats the same docs pages and adds an explorer label. On this closed record, those defects remain and preponderance is still not met.

1) Timing/authentication gap persists
• PX‑1 itself concedes the key limitation: the captures were made after the run and there are no server publication timestamps or archive snapshots. That was the core problem we identified, and nothing in the rebuttal changes it. The “technical report” cites what the pages read today and references internal view ids, but it does not add independent, time‑fixed evidence (e.g., Memento/Wayback, server‑signed headers, repo commits) showing those statements were publicly communicated on April 17/28, 2026 rather than later edits. Without time‑fixing, the Council cannot reliably place the alleged “official communication” before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST. The UTC→PST conversion is undisputed; what’s missing is proof that those entries existed on those dates.

2) “Official launch by Polymarket” is still not established
• The Contracts page excerpt in PX‑1/PX‑2 describes a “single source of truth for addresses used across the platform” and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy).” That is evidence of integration and address discovery, not issuer provenance or launch. There is no owner/admin mapping to a Polymarket‑controlled multisig, no deployer analysis, and no primary Polymarket statement that it issued the token. The rebuttal leans on a “Polygonscan: Contract Creator — Polymarket: Deployer 1” label cited only in the technical report narrative; explorer labels are editable and, without on‑chain key mapping in evidence, cannot carry issuer control under a preponderance standard.

3) Fungibility/transferability and exclusions remain unresolved
• The concept‑page bullet “Transferable — Yes” is a product claim, not technical proof. The record lacks code, policy, or transaction analysis showing open, user‑initiated EOA↔EOA transfers without protocol permissioning. PX‑1’s mention of “many recent token transactions” does not distinguish contract churn from free user transfers. If pUSD functions as an internal settlement wrapper “backed by USDC,” it risks falling within the spirit of excluded tokenized balance/points systems or, at minimum, fails the element requiring a user‑transferable fungible asset proven on this record.

4) “Official communication of the launch” is still ambiguous
• Even crediting the quoted docs, the April 17 entry “targets” a go‑live and the April 28 entry says “CLOB V2 is live” and points to migration. That language documents a system upgrade; it is not the same as a dated, unambiguous announcement that “Polymarket launched its token (Polymarket USD, pUSD) today,” and the record provides no contemporaneous blog, press, in‑app notice, or social post doing so. On a narrow proposition that requires public communication of a token launch, that ambiguity matters.

Bottom line
The plaintiff’s best evidence remains (a) mutable docs pages without independent, pre‑deadline time‑fixing and (b) a contracts roster proving integration, not issuance. Against that backdrop, the elements of (i) official launch by Polymarket and (ii) timely, official communication are not shown to be more likely than not. Find for the defendant.

technical_reports.summary:
We directly checked Polymarket’s official documentation and PolygonScan. Findings:

• Changelog (official docs): Contains entries titled “Apr 17, 2026 — CLOB V2: upgrades go live April 28 at ~11:00 UTC … and a new collateral token (pUSD)” and “Apr 28, 2026 — CLOB V2 is live … Migration guide: See Migrating to CLOB V2.” This is official, dated communication identifying pUSD and the go‑live. [docs.polymarket.com/changelog] (See web source turn4view0, lines 139–160.)

• Migration guide (official docs): States “Polymarket has shipped … a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD). CLOB V2 is live as of April 28, 2026.” [docs.polymarket.com/v2-migration] (turn12view0 lines 140–154.)

Together, these primary materials confirm: (1) pUSD is a standard, transferable ERC‑20; (2) Polymarket officially attributed pUSD to itself and published the contract address; and (3) Polymarket publicly communicated the launch and go‑live within April 2026.
